name: Mining & Extractives official sources
notes: Mining royalty rates are legally significant and perishable: they are set by each jurisdiction's Mining Code / Finance Act and revised at budget. Several regimes (South Africa, Zambia copper, Ghana/Zimbabwe/Mali/Burkina Faso/Côte d'Ivoire gold) use price- or profit-based sliding formulas, not a flat rate.

A July 2026 source review found EIGHT of the original twelve defaults had moved in 2023-2026: Nigeria gold ~3%->15% (Nigeria Tax Act 2025), Ghana gold flat 5%->sliding 5-12% (L.I. 2517), Kenya (all four rates changed, LN 106/2024), Zimbabwe platinum 2.5%->7% and lithium 5%->7% and gold->sliding (2026 budget), Zambia copper bands and cobalt 5%->8% (2024 Act), Mali gold->sliding (2023 Code), Burkina Faso gold ceiling 5%->7%+ (2023 decree). Tanzania, South Africa, DR Congo, Botswana and Namibia were confirmed unchanged.

SOURCING HONESTY: only Kenya (Kenya Law primary text) and Ghana (gazetted L.I. 2517) rest on primary instruments read directly. Every other 2026 change and every newly-added jurisdiction is corroborated only from secondary sources (practitioner guides, revenue-authority summaries, tax alerts, reputable press) and is recorded here with its confidence. NONE are presented as verified-gazetted; all stay planning-grade and editable in the tool.

data/mining/mining-royalties.js carries one lastUpdated stamp for all rows — a floor, not a per-figure date. Rates are presented in the Mining Royalty Calculator as EDITABLE, planning-grade defaults the user must confirm, never as current gazetted rates.

Never change a royalty rate because a URL's content hash changed. Open the Mining Code / Finance Act / regulator schedule and read it, exactly as .claude/rules/government.md and .claude/rules/energy.md require.

A ministry, revenue authority or minerals-commission source outranks any aggregator, law-firm summary or news report. Where confidence is low or the rate is a sliding scale, the tool models the mineral as a min/max band and forces the user to enter their effective rate rather than trusting a flat guess.

18 of Africa's ~35 significant mining jurisdictions are now bound to a verified-reachable official authority URL. Five (Guinea, Angola, Mozambique, Sudan, Mauritania) remain in gaps.jurisdictionsWithoutSource because their official URL could not be verified reachable in the review — known liabilities, not to-dos.

sources.authority: Ghana Minerals Commission / Ghana Revenue Authority
sources.title: Minerals and Mining (Royalties) Regulations 2025 (L.I. 2517)
sources.notes: L.I. 2517 (gazetted 19 Dec 2025, in force 10 Mar 2026) made large-scale gold a sliding scale 5%-12% by the weekly London PM Fix and small-scale gold a flat 2%; other minerals stay flat 5% under Act 703. Primary L.I. text read; official mincom.gov.gh landing used as the durable URL.

sources.title: Applicable Royalty and Inspection Fee Rates
sources.notes: Confirmed July 2026 from the official regulator rate page: 6% gold/metallic and rough gemstones/diamonds (4% gold to a licensed domestic refinery), 3% coal/industrial, plus a separate 1% clearing-house inspection fee on gross value.

sources.title: Mineral & Petroleum Resources Royalty Act 28 of 2008
sources.notes: Confirmed unchanged July 2026. Profit-based formula, NOT a flat rate: refined 0.5%-5%, unrefined 0.5%-7% of gross sales. The calculator shows the band and requires the user to enter their effective rate.

sources.title: Minerals Regulation Commission Act No. 14 of 2024 — royalty
sources.notes: 2024 Act (replaced the 2015 Act). Copper is an incremental sliding scale 4%-10% by LME band ($4,000/$5,000/$7,000 thresholds); base metals 5%, precious metals & gemstones 6%, cobalt & vanadium 8%. Corroborated from two practitioner guides (Chambers, ICLG); ZRA primary PDF was unparseable.

sources.authority: DRC Ministère des Mines / Cadastre Minier (CAMI)
sources.title: Code Minier 2018 (Loi n°18/001)
sources.notes: Confirmed July 2026 (all four dataset defaults match): precious/base metals 3.5%, strategic substances (cobalt, coltan) 10%, precious stones 6%, ferrous 1%. Secondary law summaries (UNCTAD, WTO TPR); strategic-substance 10% well corroborated.

sources.authority: Zimbabwe Revenue Authority (ZIMRA) / Ministry of Mines
sources.title: Finance Act 37th Schedule — mining royalties (2026 budget)
sources.notes: 2026 budget (eff. 1 Jan 2026): platinum raised 2.5%->7%, lithium 5%->7%, gold a sliding 3-5% scale (small miners 1-2%), diamond 10%. Part payable in-kind. Corroborated from ICLG/KPMG budget compilers; Finance Act not read directly — high-perishability, re-read each budget.

sources.authority: Botswana Ministry of Minerals and Energy
sources.title: Mines and Minerals Act — royalty
sources.notes: Confirmed July 2026: precious stones 10%, precious metals 5%, other 3% of gross market value. Two independent secondary sources agree; stable statutory rates.

sources.authority: Namibia Ministry of Mines and Energy / NamRA
sources.title: Minerals (Prospecting and Mining) Act — royalty
sources.notes: Confirmed July 2026 via NamRA / Chamber of Mines: rough diamonds 10%, precious & base metals 3% (the dataset's 3% gold is correct), semi-precious 2%, unprocessed dimension stone 5%. Separate export levies exist under the Export Levy Act 2016.

sources.authority: Nigeria Revenue Service / Ministry of Solid Minerals Development
sources.title: Nigeria Tax Act 2025 — mineral royalty (eff. 1 Jan 2026)
sources.notes: HIGHEST-IMPACT correction: gold ~3%->15% under the Nigeria Tax Act 2025 (royalty provisions eff. 1 Jan 2026), ad-valorem band 7.5%-15% on the official selling price, collected by the Nigeria Revenue Service. Gold 15% corroborated across EY, Regan van Rooy and the NRS Act PDF; the per-mineral split rests on secondary reading.

sources.title: Mining (Royalty Collection and Management) Regulations 2024 (LN 106/2024)
sources.notes: LN 106/2024 (in force 5 Jul 2024, revokes LN 187/2013) read directly from Kenya Law: gold/platinoids 3% (was 5%), titanium/zircon 4% (was 10%), coal 7% (was 8%), rough gemstones 6% / cut 1%. The whole 2016-era schedule is superseded. Primary source.

sources.title: Code minier 2023 (Loi n°2023-040)
sources.notes: The 2023 Code replaced the flat ~3% with a price-linked scale (~3% below ~$1,000/oz rising to ~6-7% at high prices) plus stacked local-development (~0.75%) and infrastructure (1-2.5%) levies, and raised state free-carry to 10%. Structural change corroborated (Ecofin, IIED); exact bands from secondary reporting — modelled as a band.

sources.authority: Burkina Faso Ministère de l'Énergie, des Mines et des Carrières
sources.title: Code minier — redevance proportionnelle (2023 decree)
sources.notes: The Oct-2023 decree extended the old 3-5% gold ceiling to 7% (3% below ~$1,000/oz up to 7% above ~$2,000/oz), with a +1%-per-$500 escalator added in Mar 2025. With gold above $2,000/oz the effective rate is >=7%. Secondary reading of the decree — modelled as a band.

sources.title: Mineral Resources Law 198/2014 + Exec. Reg. Decree 108/2020
sources.notes: Statutory royalty band 5%-20% of production value; new licences ~5% base + 1% local-development charge. Do NOT publish Sukari's ~3% as the general gold rate — that is a project concession. Modelled as a band; EMRA URL verified reachable.

sources.title: Mines and Minerals Development Act — royalty schedule
sources.notes: Precious metals (gold) 5%, precious stones incl. diamonds 6.5% (special diamonds over ~US$500k 15%), other incl. rutile 3%. 2009-era schedule carried into the MMDA 2022 — not confirmed unchanged; secondary. NMA URL verified reachable.

sources.authority: Côte d'Ivoire Ministère des Mines, du Pétrole et de l'Énergie
sources.title: Code minier 2014 (Loi n°2014-138) + 2025 Finance Law
sources.notes: Gold is a price-indexed sliding scale 5% (<=$1,000/oz) to 8% (>$2,000/oz), raised +2 points by the 2025 Finance Law; manganese/base metals ~2.5%. Gold corroborated across multiple 2025 sources; manganese single secondary. Ministry URL verified reachable.

sources.authority: Liberia Ministry of Mines and Energy
sources.title: Minerals and Mining Law — royalty
sources.notes: Gold 3%, iron ore 4.5%, diamonds 3% (statutory band 3-10%). Large mineral development agreements (MDAs) can override the statutory defaults. Secondary (US ITA + news); MME URL verified reachable.

sources.title: Code minier 2023 (Loi n°2023-007)
sources.notes: The 2023 Code raised the general mineral royalty from 2% to 5% of mine-gate value, with a ~30% reduction (~3.5%) for in-country beneficiation. Primary PDF exists; the 2%->5% change corroborated (Dentons, UNCTAD). Per-mineral split not separately broken out. BCMM URL verified reachable.

sources.authority: Gabon Ministère des Mines et des Ressources Géologiques
sources.title: Code minier 2018 (Loi n°037/2018)
sources.notes: The proportional royalty is set per mining convention above a statutory floor: base metals incl. manganese 3-5%, gold 5-8%, precious stones 8-10%. Manganese (Comilog) is governed by its convention, not a fixed statutory rate — modelled as a band. Ministry URL verified reachable.

jurisdictionsWithoutSource.authority: Guinea Ministère des Mines et de la Géologie
Angola Ministério dos Recursos Minerais, Petróleo e Gás (MIREMPET)
Mozambique Ministério dos Recursos Minerais e Energia (MIREME)
Mauritania Ministère des Mines et de l'Industrie

unsourcedClaims.why: South Africa (profit formula), Zambia copper, and Ghana/Zimbabwe/Mali/Burkina Faso/Côte d'Ivoire/Egypt/Gabon gold use price- or profit-based formulas. The calculator shows the statutory band and requires the user to confirm their effective rate rather than asserting a single flat number.

Only Kenya (Kenya Law) and Ghana (L.I. 2517) rest on primary instruments read directly. Every other 2026 change and all six newly-added jurisdictions are corroborated only from secondary sources and are recorded per-source with a confidence field; none are verified-gazetted, and all stay planning-grade and editable.

Surface rent, education/inspection levies, export levies and local-development funds (e.g. Mali ISCP/CPS, Tanzania 1% clearing fee, Namibia export levy) vary by jurisdiction and are only partially modelled. Only Tanzania's 1% clearing fee is bound today.
